stories.headline: Denver man arrested on suspicion of soliciting child prostitution after leaving notes on high school students' cars
stories.summary:
Denver police arrested Eric Douglas McCoy, 54, on March 30 after he allegedly left notes soliciting prostitution on students' cars in a Target parking lot near Northfield High School. McCoy faces two charges of soliciting child prostitution and one charge of attempted sexual assault, according to a Denver Police Department news release.

An undercover detective posing as a 15-year-old girl responded to an encrypted email address listed in the notes, and McCoy allegedly sent explicit messages offering alcohol in exchange for sexual acts, according to an arrest affidavit obtained by The Denver Post. The Denver Police Department declined to identify the suspect by name, citing concerns about pre-trial publicity, but McCoy was the only person confirmed arrested on such charges in Denver on that date.

McCoy was previously convicted of attempted child sexual assault in Jefferson County in February 2009 and, as of Tuesday, did not appear on Colorado's sex offender registry. Police believe other young people may have been contacted and are asking anyone with information to call the Denver Police Human Trafficking Unit tipline at 720-913-6060.

stories.headline: Man shot in neck in apparent random act of violence while riding scooter in downtown Denver
stories.summary:
A man was shot in the neck while riding a Lime scooter through downtown Denver late Friday night after a passenger in a white Chevrolet SUV became confrontational and opened fire at a stoplight near 18th Street and Stout Street. The victim, Nico Francis, said the shooter made eye contact with him, exited the vehicle to confront him, and then fired at close range as the car drove away, striking Francis in the neck and causing the bullet to travel down through his shoulder.

Francis, who temporarily lost use of his right arm and nearly lost consciousness at the scene, was transported to a hospital by ambulance after a bystander applied pressure to his wound and helped him call 911. He remains unable to use his arm and faces at least a month out of work, with uncertainty about long-term damage.

Anthony Apodaca, 31, was arrested shortly after the shooting when officers spotted a white Chevrolet Tahoe matching the suspect vehicle description near the scene and conducted a traffic stop. Apodaca faces charges related to the shooting, which Denver police and the victim describe as an apparently random act of violence.

stories.headline: Man sentenced to 5 years in prison for Denver hit-and-run that killed pedestrian
stories.summary:
Calistro Ortega-Uribe, 50, was sentenced Monday to five years in prison for fatally striking a pedestrian near North Quebec Street in Denver last August and fleeing the scene. Ortega-Uribe pleaded guilty in February to attempting to leave the scene of an accident involving death as part of a plea deal that dropped felony charges of vehicular homicide by DUI and leaving the scene of an accident involving death.

The victim, 59-year-old Nicholas Teegarden, died from blunt force injuries sustained in the crash on Aug. 16, 2025, near the intersection of Quebec Street and Interstate 70, according to the Denver Office of the Medical Examiner. His death was ruled an accident.

The case was adjudicated in Denver District Court. The sentencing brings to a close a case that drew attention to pedestrian safety and hit-and-run enforcement in the city.

stories.headline: Commerce City man charged with vehicular homicide after crash on I-270 killed girlfriend
stories.summary:
Brandon Joseph Sargent-Zuniga, 22, of Commerce City, was arrested March 28 and charged with vehicular homicide by reckless driving and leaving the scene of an accident involving death after investigators determined he struck two vehicles while attempting to pass a semitrailer on the shoulder of Interstate 270 in January. His girlfriend, 23-year-old Krista Parr, was fatally injured in the crash and was found unconscious in the back seat of his vehicle during a subsequent traffic stop, where she was declared dead.

Semitrailer camera footage contradicted Sargent-Zuniga's claim that another vehicle had brake-checked him, showing instead that he had driven onto the shoulder to pass the truck and struck both the parked RV and the semitrailer. Investigators also noted the vehicle appeared to be racing another car before the crash, though drugs and alcohol were not believed to be factors.

Sargent-Zuniga posted $50,000 surety bail on April 1 and is next scheduled to appear in Adams County District Court on April 22 for a preliminary hearing. The case drew initial attention in January when Parr was found dead during what appeared to be a routine traffic stop.

stories.headline: Colorado legislature passes expanded water quality protections for mobile home park residents
stories.summary:
Both chambers of the Colorado state legislature have passed House Bill 26-1145, a measure that strengthens water quality standards for mobile home parks and sends the bill to Gov. Jared Polis for his signature. Advocates describe the legislation as an upgrade to the Mobile Home Water Quality Act of 2023, which launched mandatory state testing that revealed approximately 9% of parks had water unsafe to drink and 13% had borderline-quality water.

The new bill closes gaps in existing law by addressing secondary water standards — covering odor, taste, discoloration and household usability — in addition to primary health safety standards. It empowers the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment's Water Quality Control Division to issue remediation orders for welfare-related violations and strengthens notice requirements.

The bill also significantly raises financial penalties for noncompliant park owners, allowing civil fines of up to $10,000 per violation plus an additional $5,000 per month after the first 30 days, as well as cease-and-desist orders. Advocates credited years of investigative reporting and community organizing for advancing the measure.

stories.headline: Sheridan schools reopen with substitute staff as teacher strike enters second week
stories.summary:
Sheridan School District No. 2 reopened all five of its schools Tuesday for the first time since educators walked off the job April 1, with bus transportation and food services resuming normal operations. Despite the reopening, teachers remained on the picket line, with the Sheridan Educators Association continuing its strike over new contracts, better pay, and what union members describe as retaliatory disciplinary practices by district administration.

District Superintendent Dr. Gionni Thompson said the district is working to staff classrooms with temporary employees while continuing negotiations with the union to reach a resolution. Teachers and support staff say employees have been disciplined or terminated for speaking out about district issues, and the union is demanding the district rescind practices outlined in what it calls Policy H.

The SEA told Denver7 that the district is paying substitute teachers a daily rate higher than what some full-time teachers earn, though district officials had not responded to that claim as of Tuesday. Attendance at reopened schools was reported to be low, with one striking educator saying the situation highlights the cost of not resolving the contract dispute.

stories.headline: Goat Trail Fire breaks out near Boulder, prompts evacuation warning for Sunshine Canyon area
stories.summary:
The Goat Trail Fire has burned approximately 1.7 acres near Boulder, prompting an evacuation warning for residents in areas near Hawthorne Avenue, Linden Drive, Broadway and Sunshine Canyon. The fire's location near the urban-wildland interface in the Sunshine Canyon corridor raises concerns given the region's ongoing dry conditions and low snowpack.

Fire officials had not yet released details on the cause of the blaze or containment status as of the report. The evacuation warning urges residents in the affected zone to be prepared to leave on short notice.

The Goat Trail Fire comes as Colorado faces heightened wildfire risk this spring following a season of record-low snowpack and warm temperatures across much of the state.

stories.headline: Whataburger applies to open first Denver-area location in decades near Outlets at Castle Rock
stories.summary:
Whataburger has submitted a land use application to Douglas County for a new restaurant site near the Outlets at Castle Rock, which would mark the Texas-based chain's first metro Denver location in decades. The application, filed in November 2025, proposes construction on a 1.53-acre lot adjacent to a Chipotle and across from a Sam's Club on Promenade Parkway.

The site sold in March for $1.5 million, up from a 2025 assessed value of $786,038, according to Douglas County property records. If approved, the restaurant would operate 24 hours a day, seven days a week.

Whataburger currently operates nine restaurants in southern Colorado, including locations in Colorado Springs, Fountain, Monument and Pueblo, but has not had a presence in the Denver metro area for many years. The Castle Rock application is pending county approval.

stories.headline: Colorado gas prices could surge to $4.50 amid U.S.-Iran tensions, experts warn
stories.summary:
Energy experts are warning that gas prices in Colorado could climb to $4.50 per gallon as escalating U.S.-Iran tensions push up global oil costs. Denver-area prices had bucked national trends over the past week, falling approximately 11 cents per gallon while national averages rose by the same amount, but analysts caution that relief may be short-lived.

The potential price spike would have significant impact on Colorado commuters and businesses, particularly in the Denver metro area where residents rely heavily on personal vehicles. The warning comes amid broader economic uncertainty affecting consumer spending statewide.

Experts noted that any further escalation in the Middle East conflict could accelerate the timeline for price increases at Colorado pumps.

stories.headline: New Denver restaurants: Coastal Mexican spot opens in Cherry Creek, Japanese-Italian fusion coming to Larimer Square
stories.summary:
Ash & Agave, a new coastal Mexican restaurant from restaurateur Sean Huggard, opened this week in the Cherry Creek Shopping Center in the former California Pizza Kitchen space. The restaurant features wood-fired cooking, mezcal-based cocktails and dishes inspired by Mexico's Pacific coast, including wood-grilled oysters, chargrilled octopus and sea bass ceviche.

Denver-based Bonanno Concepts announced plans to open Rōmyō, a Japanese-Italian fusion restaurant, at Larimer Square in July. The 80-seat restaurant will occupy the historic Miller Building at 1401 Larimer St. and will feature a kaiseki-inspired bar and outdoor patio.

Additionally, Broken Bow Western Bar and Dance Hall opened April 3 at 2201 Lawrence St. in downtown Denver, combining a country music venue and dance hall with the flagship retail store for the online Western retailer Broken Bow.

stories.headline: Imperial Chinese, a Denver institution for nearly 40 years, closes unexpectedly on South Broadway
stories.summary:
Imperial Chinese Seafood Restaurant and Lounge, a fixture on South Broadway since 1995, has closed after a new owner's plans to expand it into a franchise failed to materialize. Founder Johnny Hsu sold the restaurant and its building to health-care entrepreneur Dan Dietrich, who had expressed interest in growing the Imperial concept across the Denver area and into Las Vegas, but those plans never advanced.

A sign posted on the restaurant's door cited rising operating costs and ongoing economic challenges as reasons for the closure. Dietrich did not respond to requests for comment, and the future of several additional properties he acquired for the planned Imperial Go franchise remains unclear.

The restaurant, originally opened by Johnny and Anne Hsu in 1985, was celebrated for decades as one of Denver's premier Chinese dining destinations and was featured by The Denver Post in 2011 as an example of longevity in the local restaurant industry. A lawsuit filed in Arapahoe County District Court by a landlord claiming more than $100,000 in unpaid rent from Dietrich is ongoing.

stories.headline: Avalanche defeat Blues to reach 51 wins, extend Central Division lead to eight points
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche extended their impressive run with a win over the St. Louis Blues, pushing their season win total to at least 51 and extending their lead atop the Central Division to eight points. The victory came with coach Jared Bednar operating at reduced forward depth after losing a key player approximately 25 minutes into the game.

The win builds on the Avalanche's recent milestone of becoming the first NHL team to reach 50 wins this season, a benchmark the team had set as a training camp goal. Colorado's performance in the Central Division has been dominant throughout the second half of the season.

The Avalanche continue to position themselves as a top Stanley Cup contender heading into the final stretch of the regular season, with the division lead providing a significant cushion as playoff seeding takes shape.

weather.notable: About 14 degrees above the average high for this time of year!

joke.joke: Marriage tip: if your wife says 'Do whatever you want,' do not, under any circumstances, do whatever you want.

topStory.headline: U.S. and Iran Agree to Two-Week Ceasefire; Fighting Continues in Lebanon, Bahrain Struck
topStory.summary:
The United States and Iran reached a two-week ceasefire agreement late Tuesday, with President Trump announcing the deal shortly after midnight EDT and calling it a "big day for World Peace." The ceasefire is conditioned on Iran immediately reopening the Strait of Hormuz and halting all attacks on the U.S., Israel and regional partners. Pakistan, which served as a mediator alongside Egypt, Turkey and Saudi Arabia, confirmed the agreement.

Both governments claimed the outcome as a victory. Trump said on Truth Social that the U.S. would assist with shipping in the Strait of Hormuz, which carries roughly 20 percent of the world's daily oil supply, and predicted large economic gains from its reopening. Iran-backed militias in Iraq, operating under the banner of the Islamic Resistance in Iraq, announced a corresponding two-week suspension of attacks on what they described as "enemy bases" in the region, and Iraq subsequently reopened its airspace, which had been closed due to the conflict.

The ceasefire does not extend to Lebanon, where Israeli forces continued strikes Wednesday, killing at least four people in the coastal city of Tyre. Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu's office confirmed Israel supports Trump's decision to suspend strikes against Iran but stated explicitly that the agreement does not cover the ongoing war with Hezbollah. Lebanon's army warned displaced civilians against returning to the country's south as Israeli attacks persisted, and Hezbollah had not issued a public statement as of Wednesday.

Explosions were reported in Bahrain's capital Manama in the hours following the ceasefire announcement. Bahrain's interior ministry said civil defense crews extinguished a fire at a facility it attributed to what it called "the sinful Iranian aggression," and two people were slightly injured by shrapnel from an intercepted Iranian drone in the Sitra area. The King Fahd Causeway connecting Saudi Arabia and Bahrain, closed briefly over concerns about incoming fire, was later reopened.

United Nations Secretary-General António Guterres called on all parties to abide by the ceasefire terms and comply with international law, including the protection of civilians and civilian infrastructure. The International Air Transport Association warned that even with the Strait of Hormuz open, jet fuel supplies and prices could take months to normalize due to damage to refining capacity in the region. Pope Leo XIV, the first American pope, also called on "all people of goodwill" to reject war and pursue peace, referencing Trump's earlier threats against civilian infrastructure in Iran as violations of international law.
